images.caption:
Figure 1.Illustration of the proposedPSRDframework.PSRDfirst activates the intrinsic hallucination discrimination capacity of LVLMs through the uncertainty signals to train a lightweight phase-wise reward model.
Then the reward model monitors the response online to provide on-the-fly reward signals, enabling dynamic, targeted intervention during the decoding process.

Figure 2.Characterization of dynamic hallucination patterns across and within generation phases. The upper panel illustrates the average hallucination rate across consecutive phases, while the lower panel depicts the word-level hallucination rate within each phase. The error rate peaks at the onset of each semantic segment, identifying these transitions as critical junctures for hallucination emergence.

Table 1.Comparison with the state-of-the-art methods for the generative hallucination mitigation tasks across three benchmarks.Boldindicates the best result among post-hoc methods, andunderlineindicates the second-best.

Figure 3.Quantitative results of phase-specific hallucination mitigation.
By intervening at critical phase junctions, PSRD suppresses hallucination propagation and achieves a lowerRaccR_{\mathrm{acc}}than LLaVA-1.5-7B and M3ID.
At index 0, PSRD and M3ID exhibit identical hallucination rates.

Figure 4.Distribution of reward model output scores under different training settings. We visualize the density of CLIP similarity scores for positive and negative samples when training with and without theℒHC\mathcal{L}_{\text{HC}}Loss. IncorporatingℒHC\mathcal{L}_{\text{HC}}leads to better separation between the two distributions, reflected by a reduced overlap ratio, indicating improved discriminability of the reward model.

Figure 5.Trade-off between hallucination mitigation effectiveness and efficiency of the proposedPSRDon theObject HalBench.
We vary the thresholdτ\tauin Sec.3.2and evaluate the performance of hallucination degree (CHAIR score, lower is better) and the average time consumed per question (in seconds, lower is better).
The compared methods areLLaVA1.5-7B(Liuet al.,2024),M3ID(Faveroet al.,2024)and AVISC(Wooet al.,2024).

Appendix BDetails for Uncertainty-Guided Data Generation

images.caption: Figure 6.Reward score as a function of the intervention strengthα\alphawithin the bounded probing interval. PSRD does not require global monotonicity in the full decoding space; it only relies on a local empirical trend and uses a fallback branch when projection is unreliable.
Figure 7.Relative fluency / perplexity change under different intervention strengthsα\alpha. The figure illustrates the reward–fluency trade-off during decoding.

Figure 8.Justification of the default loss weights by magnitude balancing at the early training stage.
The three curves correspond to the Hallucination Consistency loss, the Discriminative Alignment loss, and the Margin Enforcement loss, respectively.
Before weighting, the consistency-related term dominates due to its larger numerical scale.
After applying the default weights(λ1,λ2,λ3)=(1.0,2.4,0.1)(\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2},\lambda_{3})=(1.0,2.4,0.1), the magnitudes become more balanced, which helps stabilize multi-objective training.

Figure 9.Distribution of pseudo-label confidence scores produced by the self-evaluation process. The overall separation between grounded and hallucinated samples supports the use of these signals as weak supervision.
